questions.question: Were the confirmation pairs held out from the task selection decision?
questions.evidence: Pairs A/B selected the 16 tasks; Pairs C/D were executed afterward and were not used to change the set.

questions.question: Did the holdout pairs reverse backend order on the same host?
questions.evidence: Pair C ran Lexmount then Local; Pair D ran Local then Lexmount, both at c6 on the same Linux host.

questions.question: Did every holdout arm produce 16 trajectories and 16 valid judge records?
questions.evidence: All four holdout arms are 16 planned / 16 trajectory / 16 judged. Pair D Lexmount task 52 required an isolated GPT-5.4 text-only rejudge after a multimodal content-policy false positive.

questions.question: Was a judge transport failure counted as a browser failure?
questions.evidence: The synthetic task 52 record was removed, 15 valid records were preserved, and only task 52 was rejudged with the same model and non-visual evidence. Synthetic failures remaining: 0.

questions.question: Did the nine selected stable directional tasks all remain perfectly stable?
questions.evidence: Holdouts confirmed 16/18 expected directional observations. Task 217 became both-failed in Pair D; task 292 became both-success in Pair D.

questions.question: Do the data support site-specific directional effects?
questions.evidence: Four tasks were Lexmount-only in all four repeats and three were Local-only in all four repeats; stable holdout confirmation was 90.0% and 87.5%, respectively.

questions.question: Do the data prove Lexmount is generally more accurate than Local Chrome?
questions.evidence: The set was deliberately enriched using prior outcomes. The 38/64 versus 29/64 all-repeat total is selection-biased and is not a population estimate.

questions.question: Do failure categories and log signatures prove the browser-level cause?
questions.evidence: They are diagnostic associations. They separate access/site symptoms from runner/control symptoms but do not isolate proxy, browser, website, model, or harness causality.

questions.question: Was a fixed Lexmount session TTL demonstrated?
questions.evidence: Observed control failures varied in duration and the configured task timeout was 600 seconds. No session TTL was set by the provider configuration.

questions.question: Did Lexmount leak sessions or fail session creation in the holdouts?
questions.evidence: Both Lexmount arms: baseline 0, observed max 6, final 0, residual 0, monitor errors 0, session_create signals 0.

questions.question: Did Local Chrome trigger the cgroup or host-memory guard?
questions.evidence: Both Local arms had null guard_triggered; minimum host available memory remained above 53.5 GiB. Existing unrelated Chrome processes were not modified.

questions.question: Are A/B and C/D operationally identical repeats?
questions.evidence: A/B were embedded in c10 64-task runs; C/D were focused c6 16-task runs. The task identities match, but workload context differs.

questions.question: Could same-night website or model drift still affect results?
questions.evidence: Counterbalancing reduces order bias but does not remove temporal changes in public websites, routing, or model sampling.

questions.question: What is the strongest next falsification test?
questions.evidence: Verify 64 simultaneous sessions directly, then run a c16/c32/c64 ladder while tracking quality, session lifecycle, runner resources, and Local Chrome guard behavior.
